# Transcrição: 08-Other Due Diligence

- **Fonte:** 08-Other Due Diligence
- **Duração:** 05:50
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Welcome back to the deal execution module. In this next lesson, we're going to cover all the other types of due diligence that didn't fit into the previous sections. This will be one of the shorter lessons, but no less important. Because especially when you're doing your first deal, you want to be prepared for as many different things as possible that might come up after you close on it. And that's why doing additional due diligence is so important. We've already covered financial due diligence, which involves looking at things like income statements, cash flow, tax, all those sort of documents and information. Then we moved on to commercial due diligence in this lesson. We delved into the commercial aspects of the business and its sector, looking at the market, any competition, the customer base, as well as products, services, unique selling propositions, all those things. Next came legal due diligence. We talked about identifying the business structure such as LLC, C Corp, S Corp, partnership, whatever it is, etc. We also looked at things like permits, insurance, and employment agreements or employment benefits, things of this nature. Then we did our financier due diligence, where the institution looking at getting us the loan had their own things they wanted to check out. And now I want to go over the other two items, cultural due diligence and operational due diligence. When you do your cultural due diligence, it means you're looking at what the organization says are its values, as well as how those values are lived out at all levels of the organization, which means you need to talk about habits, what's currently going on within the business, how the people are living out those values each and every day. You want to look at the company culture of the business you're planning to acquire and think about how does that line up with your vision. If you come in and you have a totally misaligned vision for what the employees in the business have been used to, you could end up losing key employees quickly. And that would inhibit you from operating the business once you take it over. Here's an example. If a company places high value on workplace flexibility, it may be culturally acceptable to work from home occasionally, be late, or whatever the terms are. The organization may even offer financial incentives for doing so, such as offering a stipend, meaning a workspace rent is lower with more employees working from home. That's a financial and cultural decision that impacts several aspects of the operations, while also recognizing what and where employees place value, which means if you take over the business and you want employees to come to work every day, it may culturally disrupt the organization, but it also might require you to rent more space for your employees to work from. This is something you'll need to keep in mind for all of your deals. Will you be able to fulfill your vision for the business with the business operating the way it currently is? Most likely not. And if you make

## [03:01 - 05:50]

changes to the company culture, how is it going to impact the employees and performance of the business? All right, now let's talk a little bit about operational due diligence. When you perform operational due diligence, you're measuring how the business turns inputs into outputs. We'll look at three key items here. First, you'll do an initial assessment of the target company's operations. You need to find out how well the operations of the target company fit with you as the buyer and think about any synergies or conflicts that may exist. Next, you'll want to look for any quick wins that can generate value. Do you see anything that could impact the business operations positively within a small timeframe and potentially at a low cost? Then you want to think about what kind of investment is going to be required to bring the target company operations to the level you desire. And is the business running at near capacity using old technology or is the current process too expensive? After we do an initial assessment, we'll want to do a document review as well. Now, depending on the business you're acquiring, there might not be a lot of operational documents at all. If there are, you're looking for anything showing internal process procedure systems, compliance records, relevant licenses, subscription documentation, and reviewing any key performance indicators of the operation against industry competitors or past results. You should also review the company's tech stack and make note of the intellectual property that they use to conduct their business. Next, you'll want to do an onsite visit. You may have already visited the business and seen the offices, facilities, etc. But you'll want to do a final assessment of the company's quote as is operations. A final check of what the condition of any factories, offices, generally the businesses conditions are, especially when it comes to technology, equipment, machinery, etc. You should also interview any relevant players upstream and downstream in the supply change such as suppliers, distributors, service providers, bankers, etc. You can also take a greater note of the onsite operations and culture when you visit. Is it structured or ad hoc? Is there a manager or everyone working autonomously? Any health or safety issues that you spot that you think need to be addressed or anything that you think a remarkable organization should do to run efficiently, effectively, and profitably. So those are three key things when it comes to operational due diligence. And trust me, there are so many other things you may want to do, depending on the kind of business that you're buying. But this just gets you started. This is another reason you want to have an expert by your side, by the way. And if you can afford it, multiple experts. I hope this is helpful and gives you some things to think about while you pursue your deal. So that's it for this lesson. And we'll see you on the next one. Bye for now.
